passage: In an experiment designed to study conformity, psychologist Solomon Asch asked participants to judge which of three lines on a card matched a reference line in length. The task was visually unambiguous—the correct answer was obvious. However, each participant was seated with a group of confederates who had been instructed to give the same wrong answer aloud before the participant responded. In approximately 75% of the trials, at least some participants gave the incorrect answer to match the group consensus, despite contradicting their own perceptions. When one confederate broke from the group and gave a correct answer, participants' conformity dropped substantially.
question: Based on the passage, what can most reasonably be inferred about the role of the dissenting confederate in the Asch experiment?

choices.A: Having even a single ally who deviates from the group's incorrect consensus significantly reduces an individual's tendency to conform.
choices.B: Participants only gave correct answers when the dissenting confederate directly encouraged them to trust their own perceptions.
choices.C: The presence of the dissenting confederate proved that participants had known the correct answer all along and were simply pretending to be confused.
choices.D: A dissenting confederate was unnecessary to reduce conformity because most participants eventually gave correct answers regardless.

explanation: When a confederate broke from the group, 'participants' conformity dropped substantially.' This directly supports the inference that even one dissenting voice reduced conformity significantly. A accurately states this. B adds a detail (direct encouragement) that is not in the passage—the passage only says the confederate gave a correct answer, not that they encouraged others. C is unsupported—the passage provides no evidence that participants were 'pretending'; the experiment design was about social pressure overriding accurate perception. D contradicts the passage, which says approximately 75% of trials showed some conformity, not that conformity eventually dissolved without intervention.
